title: Understanding Brittle and Splitting Dog Nails: Causes and Triage
meta_desc: Learn why your dog's nails are splitting, when to see a vet (Low Urgency), and how photos help with diagnosis and home care.
content_html:
What are Brittle or Splitting Nails in Dogs?

Brittle or splitting nails, medically known as onychodystrophy, occur when a dog's claws become dry, weak, and prone to cracking or peeling in layers. This condition can affect a single nail due to trauma or multiple nails due to underlying health issues. When a nail splits, it can expose the sensitive quick—the living tissue inside the nail containing nerves and blood vessels—which can be quite painful for your pet.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for brittle or splitting nails is generally Low. While the condition can be uncomfortable and may cause your dog to lick their paws frequently, it is rarely a life-threatening emergency. You should schedule a routine veterinary appointment to investigate the cause. However, if there is profuse bleeding that does not stop with pressure, or if your dog is showing signs of extreme pain and refusing to walk, you should contact a clinic sooner for advice.

How a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photo of the affected nails can be immensely helpful for your veterinary team. A photo allows the vet to see the specific pattern of the split, check for signs of infection at the nail bed, and assess whether the quick is exposed or damaged. This visual information helps the triage staff determine how quickly your dog needs to be seen and provides a baseline for monitoring the condition as it heals.

faqs.question: What common conditions cause brittle nails?
faqs.answer: Common causes include nutritional deficiencies (such as a lack of omega-3 fatty acids), fungal infections, or immune-mediated diseases like Symmetrical Lupoid Onychodystrophy (SLO).

faqs.question: Can I trim a split nail myself?
faqs.answer: If the nail is dangling or split far from the quick, you may be able to carefully trim the loose piece. However, if the split extends into the quick, it is best to let a veterinarian handle it to prevent pain and further injury.

faqs.question: How can I strengthen my dog's nails?
faqs.answer: Consult your vet about adding biotin or fish oil supplements to your dog's diet, and ensure their nails are trimmed regularly to prevent snags and trauma.
